title: Stage 4 — The Declining Phase
subtitle: The stage that destroys portfolios — avoid it completely

lessons.title: The Breakdown Signal
lessons.visual_caption: TCS — The 30-week SMA rolls over and begins declining. Price breaks below the Stage 3 support floor. Stage 4 has begun. Notice how the SMA becomes a ceiling rather than a floor.
lessons.content:
Stage 4 is the structural downtrend. The stock falls below its declining 30-week average and keeps falling.

Trend Direction: A structural downtrend — lower lows and lower highs.

Price Action: The breakdown occurs when the weekly close drops clearly below the Stage 3 support floor. This is the mirror image of the Stage 2 breakout.

The Master Line: The 30-week SMA rolls over and slopes clearly downward. Price stays below this falling line. What used to be a support floor is now a resistance ceiling.

Volume: Unlike a Stage 2 breakout, a breakdown does not need high volume to be valid. High volume only makes it more dangerous. A stock falls from a simple absence of buyers.

Look at the TCS chart: after Stage 3 distribution, the 30-week SMA rolled over and the stock broke below support. The falling SMA became a ceiling above every rally attempt.

→ Breakdown = weekly close below Stage 3 support floor
→ 30-week SMA rolling over and declining
→ Former support becomes resistance
→ No buyers = stock falls on its own weight

lessons.title: Strict Exit and Avoidance Rules
lessons.visual_caption: Infosys in Stage 4 — The 30-week SMA is declining and acting as resistance above price. Every rally attempt fails at the SMA. This is what Stage 4 looks like.
lessons.content:
Stage 4 has the strictest rules of all four stages. There are no exceptions.

The Exit Trigger: Sell 100% of any remaining shares the week price closes below the Stage 3 support floor. Do not wait for a bounce. Do not wait for confirmation. Sell.

The Stop-Loss Rule: If you missed selling in Stage 3 or at the breakdown, sell now. A Stage 4 stock can lose 50%, 70%, 80% or more. Every week you wait costs more capital.

The Buying Rule: Never buy a Stage 4 stock. Do not buy the dip — a cheap stock can always get far cheaper under a falling 30-week SMA.

Never average down on a loser. Averaging down on a Stage 4 stock turns a manageable loss into a catastrophic one.

Why Stage 4 is so dangerous:
→ It can last months or years
→ It looks like it should bounce at every level
→ There is always a reason to hope (earnings, news, analyst calls)
→ But a stock below a falling 30-week SMA is in an institutional downtrend — until it is not

The only correct action in Stage 4:
→ If you own it: sell everything immediately
→ If you do not own it: ignore it completely
→ Wait for Stage 1 to form, then Stage 2 to begin

→ Sell 100% at the Stage 3 support break
→ Never buy the dip in Stage 4
→ Never average down on a Stage 4 stock
→ Wait for the full cycle to reset to Stage 1

questions.question: What confirms a stock is entering Stage 4?
options: It moves sideways in a tight channel
It closes cleanly below the Stage 3 support floor
It rises more than 10% above its average
It stays quiet on low volume for three years
questions.explanation: A clean close below the Stage 3 support floor shows sellers have overwhelmed buyers, ending the top and starting the structural decline.

questions.question: What is a key difference between a Stage 2 breakout and a Stage 4 breakdown in terms of volume?
options: A Stage 2 breakout needs no volume
A Stage 4 breakdown needs triple volume to be real
A Stage 4 breakdown is valid even on low volume
There is no difference between them
questions.explanation: Upside breakouts require buying commitment (high volume); downside breakdowns can happen on low volume from a simple absence of buyers. Never wait for high volume before exiting.

questions.question: What should you do if you own a stock that breaks down into Stage 4?
options: Buy more shares to lower your average cost
Hold until the next earnings report for clarity
Remove the chart and wait five years
questions.explanation: Stage 4 stocks trend down for long stretches. Selling immediately stops a small loss from becoming a capital-destroying one. There are no exceptions to this rule.

questions.question: Why is buying the dip dangerous in a Stage 4 stock?
options: The stock is overextended to the upside
A Stage 4 downtrend can keep falling much further than most expect
Volume will automatically double if you buy
questions.explanation: Cheap stocks in Stage 4 can always get cheaper. Under a declining 30-week SMA, every rally attempt tends to fail at the falling average acting as resistance. Under Weinstein's rules you only buy verified Stage 2 uptrends.
